sentences.text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
I'm Anya Kagan Kornhauser.
Kagan is my maiden name, K-A-G-A-N.
Kornhauser is my married name, K-O-R-N-H-A-U-S-E-R.
Yeah.
Actually, my name is Hannah Kagan, but during the war I acquired the name, Anya, A-N-Y-A.
I went by the name Hannah.
I was born August 20, 1925.
Don't worry.
[LAUGHS]
I was born in Ostrog nad Horyniem, in the Polish Ukraine.
O-S-T-R-U-G.
And the N-- and-- means it's on the river of Horyn.
In the Polish Ukraine, because the Ukraine was a republic in Russia.
And now they are independent.
And they were never an independent country.
But when the Germans came in, in-- in 1941, they promised them an independent Ukraine if they would help them with the Jewish problem.
And they were only too happy to oblige.
No.
Right, Ukraine, yeah.
To the Russian border.
Yeah.
Yes, it was.
We-- they killed 15,000 Jews there.
The total, I would say must have been about 25,000.
The majority--
Yeah, Jewish.
I went to school in Ostrog.
And I went to public school.
And then I-- I went to the different school called [NON-ENGLISH].
But then when the Russians came in, I went to a third school.
And they set us back two years.
And that was a school that I learned-- all the subject were in Ukraine, because it's the Ukraine.
I also-- there was a subject, Jewish.
And I also took two years of English.
What?
Excuse me?
They set us back, because they thought that their education was--
Yeah.
[LAUGHS]
Yes, there was.
My family-- they were traditional, but I would say a little orthodox too.
They were very kosher people.
And-- and my father used to go to-- to shul.
So he never ate before he prayed.
So I don't know what to call-- call him.
But it was a Jewish home.
Yes.
Right.
No.
Yes.
My father was a businessman.
He had a-- my-- a store, which was called [NON-ENGLISH].
It means the store of all kind of different things-- shirts, shoes, underwear, and all like that.
And my mother was in business with him.
That too, but she wasn't for a-- normally a housewife, no.
She was in business with my father.
And she also was a woman that did a lot of volunteer work-- a lot of organizations.
She was quite a lady.
Yes.
She was very active in the community.
And in those years not too many people were very active, but she was.
Yeah.
Yes, I had two brothers and one sister.
My sister survived.
She's in Israel.
One of my--
Yes.
I was the-- she was the oldest, and I was the youngest.
And my older brother was drafted into the Russian Army in 1940.
And I never saw him again.
He was-- he was stationed near Kiev.
And he-- I think he went into the first fire, but nobody ever saw him.
No.
No.
And my-- the younger brother ran away to Russia, because when the Germans attacked [SIGHS] Russia without a warning, everybody starting to run towards the Russian border.
At that time, they opened up the borders.
And there was a lot of confusion.
The planes were flying very low.
You could see the pilots.
And-- and he was debating-- should he or shouldn't he go too?
But my mother couldn't decide on the fire until my mother pushed my brother.
And she said, run, my child.
And he ran away to Russia.
How old was he at that [BACKROUND NOISE] time?
At that time, he was-- he-- he had to be about 16, I think.
No. he actually-- when he came to Russia, he was an age that he could have been drafted.
But they didn't draft him, because he worked in [NON-ENGLISH].
This was a city in Russia, and he had deferment.
So that's why they didn't take him to the army.
It's [NON-ENGLISH ].
How do I translate [NON-ENGLISH]?
[LAUGHS] The things you make bread out of-- the--
Not dough.
The--
The thing that grow.
The grain.
It was something like a mill.
But that's where they got all the stuff to make bread.
And they needed him there on-- in that.
He got the deferment.
It's called in Russian, [RUSSIAN].
OK.
[LAUGHS]
Yeah.
In the 19--
Yeah.
When you start school?
When the Germans came to Poland, I was then already 16, because when the Germans attacked Russia, I was 14 in '39.
When the--
When the German--
No.
On the--
When the Germans divided Poland in 1/2 with the Russians.
That's when I was 14.
And when then-- when they attacked Russia without a warning, by then, I was two years older.
So then I was 16.
Yeah.
Right.
And I went to Russian school, and I had a pretty normal life, except my parents were not too happy.
Because after a lifetime of being business people, they had to stay home and do nothing.
Because my father couldn't get a job, because he was a businessman.
All the wealthy were sent to Siberia, and everything they owned was confiscated.
Since we were middle class, we were forced to sell our merchandise.
And my father couldn't get a job.
To sell our merchandise from our business.
To the people in the city--
--to anybody.
They used to have like, lines near the city, and you had to sell out everything you had from the store.
Discontinue.
Yeah.
Right.
There was no more store.
That's right.
No.
No, he couldn't get a job anymore.
Nobody could.
Anybody who was a businessman couldn't get a job, because it was communism there.
Yes.
I would say that as far as anti-Semitism is concerned, I come from the worst part of the world, because the Ukrainians, we-- when they-- they were our neighbors.
We went to school with them.
We lived together.
But to them, we were always foreigners.
They never considered us citizens of Poland or the Ukraine.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
And there was a lot of anti-Semitism everyplace.
Well the Polish government, there-- there wasn't too much love between them either.
No.
As a matter of fact, after-- after they got-- they killed off all the Jews, they started to kill the Poles-- the Ukrainians.
No.
That's true.
But most of all, the Jews.
That's right.
Fairly safe, I would say.
Yeah, I went for two years to school.
Every time we sold something else from the house.
And-- and that's how we lived, because there was-- my father didn't have a job.
And for schooling, they didn't have to pay.
The Germans attacked--
--Russia-- June 22, 1941.
Oh, yes.
They-- we didn't expect it, but during the day, they attacked us without a warning.
There was such confusion.
People started to run to the border, on foot, on horses, cars, buggies, anything.
Families were trying to stay together.
We almost went too, but somehow we didn't.
And after everybody ran away, we went into hiding into a cousin's cellar.
Everybody was running.
Well, there were some.
Like, Ukrainians didn't run.
They-- they knew that the Germans are gonna be with them-- good to them.
Yes.
Correct.
Yes.
Yeah.
Correct.
Well, two days before the Germans attacked, we received a postcard from him saying, I wish I could tell you something, but I can't.
But please send me food.
Food.
But we never made it.
We started to make bundles.
We never sent it, because it was--
Yes.
And it was written in Jewish.
Yiddish.
Yiddish.
Yes.
The postcard--
--did-- told us that something that he knew and didn't tell us-- for fear, I guess.
Oh, yeah.
The day the Germans attacked, whoever ran away to Russia was there.
Other people went into hiding to different places.
And because it was a border town, there was street fights for a whole week.
And every time the Germans would open up a house and come in and see there were Jews, they would say to us, you'll be lucky if the SS won't come by.
Because we didn't know anything about that.
And there was street fights for a whole week.
And after that, the Germans took over.
And when the Germans took over, we all came out from the hiding places.
And that's when the bad things started to happen.
It didn't take a few weeks, maybe two weeks, that we were told to take only what we can carry.
They're gonna resettle us.
And they herded us up in the square.
The sick were taken out of the hospitals on stretchers.
Then we were told to leave everything on the sidewalk and start marching in the middle of the street, 'cause Jewish people were not allowed to walk on the sidewalk.
Then we noticed young Ukrainian men marching on the sidewalk with shovels on their shoulders-- singing.
When we came to a big field, there was a selection-- the young and the children to one side, the sick and the old to another.
We couldn't find my father.
I was so afraid they'll send my mother to work.
I was trying to protect her.
So I urged her to go to the sick.
And she didn't wanna go.
But she finally went.
When my sister noticed that my mother wasn't there, she started to scold me, 'Why did you let her go?' And my sister can scold.
So to get away from my sister, I went to the selection line, limping and bending down, hoping to go to the sick.
But this young Nazi officer straightened me out, and he said, 'You are young.
You can work,' and did not allow me to go to the sick.
As it turned out, the sick and the old were held back.
And the young and the children were sent home.
When I came home, I found my father.
He was hiding.
In the evening, you-- we heard shots.
Then, at midnight there was a knock on the window.
And my mother walked in with her long hair down, and only a slip on her body.
She couldn't talk.
She embraced my father for a long time.
In the morning, she told us what happened.
When we left, they were told to undress, put your hair down, just in case she has something hidden there.
And the Ukrainian dug graves to put 10 in 1.
They set them in lines, 10 to each grave.
When they killed 'em and buried them, while the ground was still moving.
My aunt and my uncle were in the same grave with my mother.
Suddenly, my aunt said, 'Pearl, get up.
They are calling us.'
My mother said, 'I don't want to see.' But when she finally got up, there was a young SS man standing up on a jeep, calling them.
He asked them, 'Do you believe in God?' And when they said, yes, he let them go.
But the two men from the last three graves, were held back and shot.
And one of them was my uncle, my mother's brother.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And although my mother was killed anyway-- I have-- if she didn't come home that night, I would have never forgiven myself, because I urged her to go to the sick.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [SIGHS]
She was on top of the grave.
Like they set them in lines one person backed up between the legs of the person behind them.
But the last three graves, they didn't touch it.
As they stopped.
They used to use all kinda trick.
So they stopped, and that's when the SS man was calling them.
Just-- just before it was her turn to be shot.
Yes, and that's why she didn't want to get up when her sister started to say they're calling us.
[SIGHS]
An idea?
Because that's-- that-- they did all kinds of thing to Jewish people.
One time, they would do that, and the next time, they would grab a man with a beard and pull him in the street.
There was no special reason-- just-- at Hitler's way-- just being sadistic.
But the-- but the SS man actually saved her life.
He didn't.
So he-- that was really-- he saved the-- the-- the two graves, just And he didn't really save them.
He saved them for later.
Because he didn't-- all the people were killed but the Ukrainian who dug graves.
And the Ukrainian did the killing.
But when he called them, he asked them-- what a question to ask. 'Do you believe in God?' In other words, what you see, how can you believe in God?
And-- but then, about-- I would say about three months later, they rounded up all the young people, put 'em on trucks, took them out of the city, killed them, and buried them in one big, mass grave.
The rest were told to go home.
Because the Ukrainian got those graves ready before.
We didn't know there were graves there.
We were just told to go to a certain place.
And we were hiding.
We were hiding in an attic.
And a neighbor, who was a Polish woman, she asked this Nazi who in the Ukraine who were there, to come into this house.
She said, I'm sure they're hiding there in the attic.
They probably took the ladder with them.
And he wasn't interested.
He kept on going.
There were a lot of people there.
We had some babies there.
We had a man who was coughing.
And we were terrible frightened.
But then there was another man outside who was a Ukrainian, a neighbor, a wonderful person.
He knew we were up there.
And every time when they went away, the-- the Nazis with the-- the Poles or the Ukrainian, whoever was there, he would cough.
That was a signal to us, you can relax.
And by the end of the day, he came into the house and tell us we can go down-- but not the young people, because they killed all the young people.
So we stayed up there.
And they went down.
And my mother really felt very bad and guilty that all her friends and relatives didn't have any children anymore.
And she still had her two daughters.
So we used to stay in the house a lot.
But then a ghetto was formed.
And everybody would go to work.
And if there wasn't much work, and if you didn't work, you didn't get any bread.
And I didn't have a job.
So my mother was terribly afraid they'll take me away.
So one day, I went to the Judenrat.
It was a-- Judenrat's a council of elders that was elected to run the ghetto-- to see if there was any work available.
And I saw some people signing up to go pick hops, 12 kilometers from the city.
So a few of my friends and I signed up.
I worked there from sunrise to sundown, eating only potato soup.
And that's when I got very sick with dysentery-- so crippled with cramps-- I couldn't work.
So they sent me back to the ghetto.
And just because of that, I survived.
Because when I came home, and I was very sick-- so I don't know-- or my mother got a doctor.
And I don't know where she got some things what to feed me with.
And this was about two days before orders came that we should all be at noon, at a certain place.
And we knew already what this meant.
By then, every family had a hiding place-- a hiding place that we used to call malina.
Malina means a raspberry.
But that was our code.
Yeah.
The ghetto was created after the second [NON-ENGLISH].
We had three [NON-ENGLISH].
The first, they killed the old and the sick.
The second one, they killed all the young people.
And the third one, when they told us to go to a certain place.
And we went into hiding then.
[NON-ENGLISH], yes, that was called an [NON-ENGLISH].
About 150 people.
And I'm the only one that survived from the 150.
And I felt-- feel guilty about that.
I say to myself, why me?
Why did I survive from all that?
And-- but if I didn't get sick with dysentery, I would have been with the 150.
